summary: FindOptions does not encode readConcern
description: [{{FindOptions.CodingKeys}} was removed|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/commit/e4c9487b976380ce61f1bd5fe81fa8a3a0a68367] in SWIFT-108 when the {{readConcern}} option was introduced, but it was missed when [{{FindOptions.CodingKeys}} was reintroduced|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/commit/5a654cf408d1edcf70dfbaecf704474459b29249] in SWIFT-63.

summary: Move BSON subtype into Binary
description: Similar to SWIFT-123, let's be consistent and nest types that are only used in conjunction with other types.

summary: WriteConcern CustomStringConvertible should return empty string on error
description:
I noticed that ReadConcern and WriteConcern have inconsistent implementations for CustomStringConvertible. If ReadConcern encoding fails, an empty string is returned. If WriteConcern encoding fails, a preconditionFailure is emitted. The Document type is also consistent with ReadConcern in returning an empty string.

While WriteConcern should never fail to encode as BSON, it should be consistent with the Document and ReadConcern implementations.

summary: Remove Document initializer that takes in a Dictionary
description:
if someone is creating a Document from a dictionary literal, they should do let doc: Document = [:] rather than let doc = Document([:]). the latter will not preserve key ordering and leads to double allocations.

if someone is trying to store String/BsonValue pairs to later put into a Document using this init, they could just use a Document to begin with. 

given the unexpected and non optimal behavior of this initializer, we should remove it

summary: Int should encode as Int32 or Int64 as needed
description:
The {{BsonValue.encode()}} implementation for Int requires that the integer's value falls within the range of a 32-bit signed integer. If Int contains a value outside of this range (possible based on the architecture), an error is raised that suggests Int64 be used instead. It should be possible for the driver to detect which BSON type is most appropriate.

I understand that when decoding from BSON, the driver converts Int32 and Int64 BSON types to a Swift Int and Int64, respectively. I assume Int was chosen for flexibility, as it jives with the [Swift documentation|https://docs.swift.org/swift-book/LanguageGuide/TheBasics.html]:

bq. Unless you need to work with a specific size of integer, always use Int for integer values in your code. This aids code consistency and interoperability.

Likewise, it's quite possible that applications will use an Int on a 64-bit platform to represent a 64-bit value and expect it to encode to BSON without error.

summary: 'swift build' - fails to download and compile dependencies
Hide bson_ts in BsonValue protocol
description: We already have an internal class wrapping a bson_t; we should make this type public and pass it around rather than the bson_t.

summary: MongoCollection::distinct() should return [BsonValue?]
description:
Per the [CRUD spec|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/crud/crud.rst], the {{distinct()}} helper should return {{Iterable<any>}} (see: [Terms|https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/crud/crud.rst#terms]). In the context of the Swift driver, this should probably be an array of optional BsonValue types (i.e. {{[BsonValue?]}}).

Currently, it looks as if the method stuffs the [{{distinct}}|https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/command/distinct/] command's response into a synthetic MongoCursor (see: [implementation|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/blob/28ca706546f713f52f2f87a7e103c31b041699a2/Sources/MongoSwift/MongoCollection%2BRead.swift#L77]). For reference, we can look to the [{{distinct}} example in the libmongoc docs|http://mongoc.org/libmongoc/current/distinct-mapreduce.html#distinct-command].

summary: Clean up linting
description:
We have a lot of minor violations/"warnings", some of them for rules I'm not sure we actually care about using. Similarly, there are some swiftlint rules that might be useful to us but are not enabled by default.

We should decide what rules we want to disable / opt in to, and then fix all violations in the codebase.

We should also considering adding "swiftlint autocorrect" to "make".

We should have travis test this

summary: Hide bson_iter_t and refactor iterator code
description: We have a public type that wraps a bson_iter_t, DocumentIterator. We should pass this around instead, and try to condense all bson_iter_t initialization and traversal related code into that class.

summary: When UUID binary subtype is provided, validate that data is 16 bytes
Allow for null document IDs in inserted and upserted documents
description:
The InsertOneResult and InsertManyResult objects do not allow for null inserted IDs.

This may not be an issue for UpdateResult, as its {{upsertedId}} field is optional; however, the lack of a accessor for "upsertedCount" (introduced in CDRIVER-2704). may make it difficult for users to determine whether an upsert actually occurred.

summary: Swift driver not found in cocoa pods nor in Swift Package Manager
description:
When trying to install the swift driver, using the cocoa pods, an error is being thrown:

 *[!] Unable to find a specification for `MongoSwift(~> 0.0.2)`*

Same happens using the Swift Package Manager, it tells the package MongoSwift does not exist.

summary: Filter tests by current server version
description: We currently run all of our tests assuming the latest version of the server. A number of YAML/JSON tests contain mechanisms for determining whether a test should be run depending on server version. We need some way to conditionally skip tests based on server version in our tests.

summary: Add a way to get current server version in tests
description: In order to complete SWIFT-94, we will need a method to retrieve the current server version. We can do this by running the buildInfo command.

summary: Support map/filter/reduce-like operations on documents
description:
You can currently use "map" on a document since it's a sequence, this returns tuples with key/value pairs. I think we should make them feel as much like dictionaries as possible. Ideas to consider:

1) Change DocumentIterator.next to return labeled values, a la [DictionaryIterator.next|https://github.com/apple/swift/blob/8d386702aec53a6342a5020a6c235a7257833459/stdlib/public/core/Dictionary.swift#L4521]. 

2) Dictionary provides a `mapValues` method that maps over the values in a dictionary and modifies them. this would be nice to support, and we could add similar `filterValues` and `reduceValues` methods. I'm imagining an API like:
{code:java}
let doc = ["a": 1, "b": 2, "c": "string", "d": nil]
let nonNil = doc.mapValues { $0 != nil } // ["a": 1, "b": 2, "c": "string"]
let strings = doc.filterValues { $0 is String } // ["c": "string"]{code}
These would at least partially alleviate current concerns about documents being append-only since removing/modifying values would be easier

summary: Check that input to Decimal128 constructor is valid
description:
We currently just assume that the string is valid and won't throw until the user tries to encode it, but we should consider checking its validity upon initialization.

Alternatively, we could add a separate initializer that does the check, or an "isValid" method.

summary: Make Document SubSequence type a Document
description:
By default, the type of a SubSequence (the type returned from Sequence protocol methods such as dropFirst, dropLast, prefix, etc) is an AnySequence<Element>. Some Swift types as override this so you get back the same type. We should consider making Document Subsequences also Documents. This would allow you to do something like:

let doc1: Document = ["a": 1, "b": 2]

let doc2 = doc1.dropFirst() // ["b": 2]

Currently, doc2 would instead be an iterator that has a single tuple value, (key: "b", value: 2). 

The standard library actually implements "slice" types to return in these cases, which are views into the original storage. Implementing some kind of a Document view/slice type that doesn't actually copy storage could be a possible future optimization.

summary: "Returns nil if the write concern is unacknowledged" in various places is inaccurate
description: All of the collection write methods (in [MongoCollection+Write.swift|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/blob/master/Sources/MongoSwift/MongoCollection%2BWrite.swift]) say the method will return nil if the write concern is unacknowledged, but none of actually check if the write concern was acknowledged. This is also true for [bulkWrite|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/blob/master/Sources/MongoSwift/MongoCollection%2BBulkWrite.swift#L12], so that case could potentially be addressed in SWIFT-147 (cc [~jmikola]).

summary: Handle setting keys that already exist in a document
description: Right now, we don't check for a key's existence before appending it to the bson_t. This leads to unexpected results for users who are trying to overwrite values in a Document. We should check for a key's existence first and if it is already there, then create a new bson_t with the value replaced.

summary: Document.isEmpty returns the opposite
description: We return whether we successfully advanced the iterator, but if we were able to advance, it's not empty. So we need to negate the result.

summary: Rename mentions of Bson to BSON
description:
For people who are used to using {{JSONEncoder}} and {{JSONDecoder}}, it may be annoying that we lowercase the -son. I mess this up myself sometimes... We could even just do these as type aliases. 

Since this will cause inconsistency between the various BSON-related names we have in the codebase, we should rename those to use {{BSON}} as their naming convention as well, so that we don't fix this developer usability problem and add a new one, where we may mistakenly write {{BSON...}} instead of {{Bson...}} and vice-versa. Code relying on the lowercase version, {{bson}} will remain as is.

summary: Use new optimized document methods for key presence, count, etc. everywhere possible
description:
Since much of the driver was written, we've introduced new methods to Document that optimize common tasks:
* Document.hasKey
* Document.isEmpty
* Document.count

However, we aren't always using these. For example, in some places we use `Document.keys.contains`, but we should really now use `Document.hasKey`. Similarly, we should prefer `Document.isEmpty` over `Document.keys.isEmpty`, and `Document.count` over `Document.keys.count`.

summary: Update Cocoapods recipe to use frameworks
description: The mobile SDKs have been updated to generate frameworks for libbson, libmongoc and mongoe. The cocoapods for MongoSwift and MongoMobile should be updated to use these frameworks, instead of building custom frameworks themselves.

summary: Fix .travis.yml to fail whenever swiftlint has errors OR warnings
description:
Currently, our {{travis}} configuration runs {{swiftlint}} and uses its exit code to determine if there are any linter issues we should be concerned about. However, {{swiftlint}} will give a successful 0 return code even if there are linter issues. This is most likely because {{swiftlint}} does not consider all issues (or warnings) to be 'serious', and therefore not a failed linter check.

We should instead parse the output of {{swiftlint}} to see if we have any linter issues.

Something that {{swiftlint}} seems to do is output a single line at the end of its execution saying how many violations it found, e.g.:
{code}
Done linting! Found 16 violations, 0 serious in 31 files.
{code}

Parsing that line may be useful for fixing this.

*EDIT*: What may be simpler, actually, is to use the {{--strict}} option to {{swiftlint lint}}, which supposedly treats warnings as errors and should therefore cause a non-zero exit code, even if nothing 'serious' is found. This seems to do the trick, but like mentioned above, is also still triggered by third party packages. As long as we exclude them, we should be safe.

summary: Use brew to install a more recent version of swiftlint on Travis
description: Currently, our Travis configuration uses an out-of-date Swiftlint that is installed already. This causes us to get some error output from Swiftlint. Since we should have access to {{brew}}, we should use it to install a more recent version of Swiftlint on our Travis builds.

summary: Driver doesn't build in Swift 4.2
description:
On OSX 10.14, the driver will not build in Swift 4.2 with the following error output. Per users on this [GitHub issue|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/issues/121#issuecomment-429968398], this is also an issue on Ubuntu and seems to be a Swift 4.2 issue rather than anything platform specific. 

I'm guessing it's something to do with how C names are getting imported to Swift but needs further investigation.

Note that we can ignore the weirdness about the version string, that is the usual strange error you get when there is a *different* libmongoc/libbson related error, which always disappears once you fix the actual error. Though I would like to get to the bottom of why that happens someday.
{code:java}usr/local/include/libbson-1.0/bson/bson-version.h:63:27: error: invalid suffix '.1' on floating constant
#define BSON_VERSION (1.13.1-dev)
                          ^
/Users/kaitlinmahar/mongo-swift-driver/Sources/MongoSwift/BSON/BSONValue.swift:468:41: error: use of unresolved identifier 'BSON_TYPE_CODE'
        if iter.currentType.rawValue == BSON_TYPE_CODE.rawValue {
                                        ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~
Darwin.ACL_TYPE_CODA:1:12: note: did you mean 'ACL_TYPE_CODA'?
public var ACL_TYPE_CODA: acl_type_t { get }
           ^
bson.BSON_BYTE_ORDER:1:12: note: did you mean 'BSON_BYTE_ORDER'?
public var BSON_BYTE_ORDER: Int32 { get }
           ^
{code}

summary: Provide a way to compare equality of BSONValues
README link for "Things to install" in Development.md is broken
description:
The {{README}} link found in {{Development.md}} is broken. We should make it just point to the fragment of the GitHub repository pertaining to the installation (https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver#first-install-the-mongodb-c-driver).

Currently, it points to https://mongodb.github.io/mongo-swift-driver/README.md#first-install-the-mongodb-c-driver, which gives a 404.

summary: Use setValue where possible rather than subscript setter
description: Some of our methods which throw are relying on the subscript setter. Since we now have a setValue method that throws, we should use this internally where possible to avoid unnecessary fatal errors.

summary: Use libbson overwrite methods for ObjectId, Timestamp, Datetime
description: libmongoc 1.13 will add support for overwriting these types in-place on a bson_t. We should use these to build on the work in SWIFT-196 that introduced overwriting other types (such as Int32, Int64) in-place.

summary: Add a throwing getValue method
description: Similar to setValue, we should add an internal getValue method that throws. This can be used in cases where the method retrieving a value from a document already throws and will make fatal errors less likely.

summary: Don't hard-code in port number for tests
description: Right now we hard-code localhost:27017 for the client in all of the tests. It would be nice if we could somehow accept a command line argument to {{make test}} that specifies the port to use.

summary: Create base class for functional tests
description: In [mongodb/mongo-swift-driver#97|https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-swift-driver/pull/97#discussion_r215792572], [~kaitlin.mahar] noted that {{getSpecsPath()}} is currently defined in an extension to XCTestCase. Rather than continue extending XCTestCase, we can introduce our own base class (e.g. MongoTestCase) that inherits XCTestCase and provides additional methods relevant to our own functional tests (e.g. getter for specs path, default database name).

summary: Make Document conform to Collection protocol
description:
The [Collection protocol|https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swift/collection] inherits from the Sequence protocol, which Document conforms to as of SWIFT-195.

Conforming to Collection will require adding startIndex and endIndex properties (firstKey and lastKey), as well as an index(after: ) (basically nextKey) method. -We should likely just convert SWIFT-195's Document+Sequence extension to be Document+Collection and have it all live there.- Given the new understandings of what we will need to do too get true conformance to a {{Collection}} (w.r.t. runtime complexities), combining the two will probably lead to a big file, so splitting it may just be more manageable and organized.

The documentation notes that "Types that conform to {{Collection}} are expected to provide the {{startIndex}} and {{endIndex}} properties and subscript access to elements as O(1) operations. Types that are not able to guarantee this performance *must document the departure*, because many collection operations depend on O(1) subscripting performance for their own performance guarantees."

We could potentially provide the startIndex and endIndex properties in O(1) if we wanted to store them in the Document. We would be unable to do that for subscript access, but it seems like it's ok to just document that departure.

summary: Integrate xcpretty into test target
description:
[xcpretty|https://github.com/supermarin/xcpretty] makes Swift test output on the command line much more readable by adding color, spacing, symbols, snippets of failing code, etc, as shown in screenshot below.

although it is not mentioned on the repo, as described [here|https://forums.swift.org/t/swift-test-output-format/14702/13], it works with Swift test output if you run {{swift test 2>&1 | xcpretty}}. the 2>&1 is necessary as test output goes to stderr. 

we should add an option to make test (-p or something) that does the necessary redirect and piping.

we should complete SWIFT-103 in tandem with this, as that also involves modifying {{make test}}, and without SWIFT-103 our test output is still quite ugly because {{--filter}} (which we must use to exclude the benchmarks) places each matching test in a separate suite, leading to very lengthy output. 

!Screen Shot 2018-11-18 at 5.20.33 PM.png!

summary: Move benchmarks into a separate project
description: Right now they are just tests in the repo, which means that to run all non-benchmark tests (what we do by default) requires filtering. Filtered tests generate a lot more output - about 8 lines per test vs 2 - as each matching test is placed in its own suite, and this makes finding failures and reading the output very difficult. We should move the benchmarks into their own repo that depends on MongoSwift.

summary: Move Document.filter into a separate extension
Fix UnsafeMutableRawPointer! deprecation warnings in Swift 4.1+
description:
Building the driver in Swift 4.1+, one gets a few deprecation warnings of the form
{code:java}/Users/kaitlinmahar/mongo-swift-driver/Sources/MongoSwift/APM.swift:444:78: warning: using '!' in this location is deprecated and will be removed in a future release; consider changing this to '?' instead
                                             contextFunc: (OpaquePointer) -> UnsafeMutableRawPointer!
                                                                                                  ^                                         ~
                                                                                                                                             ?
{code}

We should fix these. It is possible that the code will still build in 4.0 with the ?, so we should try that approach before doing inline version checks.

summary: Fix deallocate deprecation warnings in Swift 4.1+
description:
When building the driver in Swift 4.1+, one gets several deprecation warnings like this:

{code:java}
/Users/kaitlinmahar/mongo-swift-driver/Sources/MongoSwift/BSON/Document.swift:322:22: warning: 'deallocate(capacity:)' is deprecated: Swift currently only supports freeing entire heap blocks, use deallocate() instead
            document.deallocate(capacity: 1)
{code}

I am pretty sure that the new way will not work in 4.0, but we should confirm that. It seems that Xcode 10 + swiftenv's 4.0 are not playing nicely together and I can no longer build the driver with 4.0 locally to test myself...

Assuming it doesn't work with 4.0, we can fix these warnings by using inline version checks, that use the new way for Swift 4.1+. See _BSONKeyedDecodingContainer.allKeys for an example of an inline version check.

It appears that all of these cases involve an UnsafeMutablePointer<UnsafePointer<Int8>?> or an UnsafeMutablePointer<UnsafePointer<bson_t>?>, so we can likely accomplish this using 1-2 shared methods that handle freeing pointer pointers, rather than adding the conditional in every place.

summary: Internalize Document.merge method
Improve handling of BSON nulls
description:
Right now, it is difficult for a user to determine whether a key is present and set to nil, or not in the document at all. They need to check if it is contained in the keys array, which is not very intuitive and also not very performant as it iterates the entire document.

We should consider how we can make it more obvious to users that there is a difference between a key not being present and a key being present and set to nil. 

One interesting option for this is using a double-optional (BsonValue??) as the Document value type rather than a BsonValue?. This would allow us to return a true nil in the case of a missing key, and an Optional(nil) in the case of a key that is present and set to nil. This is the behavior that a dictionary of [String: BsonValue?] has. Our current behavior more aligns with that of a [String: BsonValue]. See this [blog post|https://developer.apple.com/swift/blog/?id=12] for some discussion... A downside of that would be that users basically have to double unwrap values all the time. 

Alternatively, we can just better document how all this works, and add some optimized helper method(s) for checking if a key is present and set to nil. There is a bson_has_field method on bson_t that would be useful.

summary: ObjectId should expose timestamp
description:
According to [https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/objectid.rst:]

Drivers SHOULD have an accessor method on an ObjectID class for obtaining the timestamp value.

summary: Loss of precision when initializing Date BSONValue from msSinceEpoch
description:
When using the {{msSinceEpoch}} initializer in our {{BSONValue}} extension of {{Date}}, some precision is lost. This initializer is used by the {{from}} method, so we can see the error when retrieving {{Date}} s from a document.

e.g.
{code:swift}let d: Document = ["a": Date(timeIntervalSince1970: 0.001)]
expect(d["a"] as? Date).to(equal(Date(timeIntervalSince1970: 0.001)))
{code}
Fails with:
 {{expected to equal <1969-12-31 19:00:00.0010>, got <1969-12-31 19:00:00.0000>}}

There is also a loss of precision in the {{msSinceEpoch}} computed property, as it simply truncates as opposed to rounding.

summary: Replace pre-existing test code to use the new bsonEqual Nimble matcher
Move Document conformance to ExpressibleByDictionaryLiteral and ExpressibleByArrayLiteral into extensions
description:
For all other {{protocols Document}} conforms to, we declare conformance via {{extension}}. We should do the same for these.

the reason we didn't initially was that classes must have protocol-required initializers in their declarations, and {{Document}} used to be a class. 

now that it is a {{struct}}, it's cleaner and more consistent to move these out to extensions.

summary: Move internal Document methods to a separate extension
description:
The Document struct declaration is getting to be quite lengthy (~350 lines), and contains a number of internal methods.

For readability/organizational purposes, we should pull out all the private/internal stuff into an extension, leaving just the public API and properties in the definition.

summary: Make test database name accessible from the base class
description:
Currently, we have hardcoded repetition of the test database name scattered throughout our testing code. This is pretty bad and annoying if we ever decide to use a different database name or if we want to do some functionality changes that involve doing something similar to generating a database name.

We should add a method to the testing base class introduced by SWIFT-207 that returns the testing database name when called.

We should then change these instances of hardcoded repetition in the codebase to call this new method.

summary: Add @dynamicMemberLookup to Document
description:
Swift 4.2 introduces the [@dynamicMemberLookup attribute|https://github.com/apple/swift-evolution/blob/master/proposals/0195-dynamic-member-lookup.md]. Basically, this is just syntactic sugar, allowing you to use dot notation to look up arbitrary properties on a type.

A natural place for us to use this is on documents. This will allow users to do things like the following:
{code:java}var doc: Document = ["a": 1, "b": 2]
print(doc.a) // prints 1
print(doc.x) // prints nil
print(doc.b) // prints 2{code}
Implementing this will require adding the attribute to Document, and adding a new subscript(dynamicMember: ) method that behaves identically to our existing subscript getter.

summary: Update podspecs to use frameworks provided by Platforms
